← All posts March 12, 2026

Planetary Parade: turning a probability nightmare into Euler's formula

  • math
  • puzzles
  • probability
  • geometry

Jane Street’s March 2026 puzzle, Planetary Parade, is a great example of a problem where the “obvious” approach is a trap and the right approach feels like cheating.

The setup. An alien on the spherical planet Pyrknot lives in a system with a star and six other planets whose positions each night are independently uniform in the sky. If, at a given moment, somewhere on Pyrknot all six planets are visible at once (with the star below the horizon — it has to be night), what’s the probability α\alphaα that our alien’s particular spot can see them all too? And if they build a tower that effectively extends their horizon by a surface distance r≪Rr \ll Rr≪R, the new probability is α+β⋅(r/R)\alpha + \beta \cdot (r/R)α+β⋅(r/R) — find β\betaβ.

Why the direct route fails. Each celestial body is visible from exactly half the planet — the hemisphere on its side, bounded by a great circle. So you want

P(parade)=P(¬s)⋅P(c1∣¬s)⋅P(c2∣c1,¬s)⋯P(\text{parade}) = P(\neg s) \cdot P(c_1 \mid \neg s) \cdot P(c_2 \mid c_1, \neg s) \cdotsP(parade)=P(¬s)⋅P(c1​∣¬s)⋅P(c2​∣c1​,¬s)⋯

and every conditional term is the area of an increasingly weird spherical polygon. After two or three planets this is unmanageable.

The reframe. Forget probabilities; count regions. Seven bodies (six planets + the star) means seven great circles drawn on the sphere. These circles carve the surface into patches, and every point inside a given patch sees the exact same set of bodies — cross an edge and exactly one body rises or sets. A parade region, if it exists, is one specific patch. So conditioned on a parade being possible somewhere, the alien needs to be standing in that one patch, and by symmetry the answer is 1/(number of patches)1/(\text{number of patches})1/(number of patches).

Euler’s formula for the sphere, V−E+F=2V - E + F = 2V−E+F=2, does the counting:

  • Vertices: any two great circles cross at 2 antipodal points, so V=2(72)=42V = 2\binom{7}{2} = 42V=2(27​)=42.
  • Edges: each circle is crossed by the other 6 circles at 12 points, cutting it into 12 arcs, so E=7×12=84E = 7 \times 12 = 84E=7×12=84.
  • Faces: F=2+E−V=2+84−42=44F = 2 + E - V = 2 + 84 - 42 = 44F=2+E−V=2+84−42=44.

α=144.\alpha = \frac{1}{44}.α=441​.

The tower. Raising your viewpoint pushes each visibility boundary outward by (to first order) a strip of width rrr. That’s good news six times and bad news once: the six planet horizons expand your valid region, but the star’s terminator moving means daylight encroaches — seeing the star kills the parade. Net weight: 67−17=57\frac{6}{7} - \frac{1}{7} = \frac{5}{7}76​−71​=75​ of the boundary is working for you.

The total length of all seven great circles is 7×2πR=14πR7 \times 2\pi R = 14\pi R7×2πR=14πR, and each arc borders two faces, so the expected perimeter of a face is 144⋅2⋅14πR=711πR\frac{1}{44} \cdot 2 \cdot 14\pi R = \frac{7}{11}\pi R441​⋅2⋅14πR=117​πR. The expected area gained is then

ΔA≈57⋅711πR⋅r=511πRr,\Delta A \approx \frac{5}{7} \cdot \frac{7}{11}\pi R \cdot r = \frac{5}{11}\pi R r,ΔA≈75​⋅117​πR⋅r=115​πRr,

and as a fraction of the planet’s surface area 4πR24\pi R^24πR2:

β⋅rR=511πRr4πR2=544⋅rR  ⟹  β=544.\beta \cdot \frac{r}{R} = \frac{\frac{5}{11}\pi R r}{4\pi R^2} = \frac{5}{44} \cdot \frac{r}{R} \implies \beta = \frac{5}{44}.β⋅Rr​=4πR2115​πRr​=445​⋅Rr​⟹β=445​.

Answers: α=144\alpha = \dfrac{1}{44}α=441​, β=544\beta = \dfrac{5}{44}β=445​.

The lesson I keep relearning from these puzzles: when a probability question is secretly a geometry question, uniformity plus symmetry lets you replace integration with counting. Euler characteristic as a probability tool was not on my bingo card, but here we are.
